message.content:
Starting with the document called anthropic_prompt_engineering_take_home.md.txt, I want you to review my project: the prompts, the approach, the iteration, the problems caught, the iterated process, the hypotheses, the iterations made, the improvements made, and then the ultimate summary, which was basically stopping at v4.6. I want you to analyze this entire project and give me your feedback on whether there are any major issues or red flags that should be fixed before submitting.

Essentially, I've used Sonnet for this project, and I just want Opus, which is you, to take a run at it and make sure that we didn't miss anything. Produce your report in order of severity. If there is a major issue, flag it first, then go to major 2, major 3, minor, etc., and then explain what the issue is, why it's important, and what is the risk if I don't address it. The assignment says you shouldn't be spending too much time on it, so I don't. I've already spent several hours on it, so I'm just asking you to review the assignment and what I've done, the approach, and the results. Ask me questions if the ask is not clear, and if it's clear then you can start your analysis. If you want, you can summarize my ask to make sure that you understood, and then you can start analysis. I want to make sure you're doing the analysis properly.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring or one-time remote agent ("run this every Monday", "open a cleanup PR for X in 2 weeks"), or to manage existing routines. ALSO OFFER PROACTIVELY: after you finish work that has a natural future follow-up, end your reply with a one-line offer to schedule a background agent to do it. Strong signals: a feature flag / gate / experiment / staged rollout was just shipped (offer a one-time agent in ~2 weeks to open a cleanup PR or evaluate results), a new alert/monitor was created (offer a recurring agent to triage it), a TODO/migration with a "remove once X" condition was left behind (offer a one-time agent to do the removal). Skip the offer for refactors, bug fixes, and anything that is done once it ships. Name a concrete action and cadence ("in 2 weeks", "every Monday") and only offer when the run just succeeded — do not pitch a schedule for something that has not happened yet.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
I'll summarize the ask, then start the review.

**My understanding:** You want me (Opus) to do an independent senior review of your wiki-qa-py take-home before submission. Read the assignment, the prompts, the iteration log, the hypotheses, the eval design, the failures caught, and the v4.6 stopping point. Produce a severity-ordered report (Major 1, Major 2, ... Minor) with: what the issue is, why it matters, and the risk of leaving it unfixed. Skip nice-to-haves — only flag things that could hurt the submission.

Starting the read now.

174	## Infrastructure changes
175	
176	### Eval/judge update — validated via regression test before V4.5
177	- **`eval/judge_prompt.txt`** — steps 7/8 updated: `ambiguous` evidence_condition treated differently from `insufficient`.
178	- **`eval/eval_and_scoring.md`** — same distinction applied.
179	- **`eval_cases.yaml`** — ambig-1/2 got 4th case_requirement: signoff when stating assumption.
180	- **Old versions preserved as:** `eval/judge_prompt_old.txt`, `eval/eval_and_scoring_old.md`, `eval_cases_old.yaml`
181	
182	### Eval cases (eval_cases.yaml) — 18 cases total
183	Original 10 + 8 added in V3 session.
184

186	
187	## Issue tracker summary (final state)
188	
189	| ID | Description | Status |
190	|---|---|---|
191	| I-001 | multihop-2 hallucination (Aracataca population) | resolved V2 |
192	| I-002 | silent disambiguation (ambig-1/2) | **resolved V4.6** — both full pass |
193	| I-003 | latent fill-in on truncated retrieval | **wontfix** — retrieval ceiling |
194	| I-004 | hedge+assert contradiction (noisy-1) | resolved V3; re-emerged V4 in negation form; closed V4.5 |
195	| I-005 | verbose abstention (insuff/multihop-2) | resolved V3 |
196	| I-006 | should abstention recommend sources? | **wontfix** — deliberate policy (Wikipedia-only scope) |
197	| I-007 | correct latent knowledge, unverifiable | **wontfix** — retrieval ceiling |
198	| I-008 | over-abstention on retrieval-ceiling cases (noisy-1/partial-1/noisy-2) | **wontfix** — tool ceiling; document in submission failure analysis |
199	| I-010 | V5 scope constraint over-suppressed disambiguation — documents structural trade-off | **wontfix** — V4.6 avoids V5 constraint; documented as prompt-scoping limitation |
200	
201	---
202	
203	## Key findings (cumulative)
204	
205	- **H1a confirmed:** V0/V1 bypass search on familiar questions. V1.5 fixed.
206	- **H1 (claim-level grounding):** V2 fixed multihop-2; V3 closed hedge+assert loophole. CV dimension isolates this.
207	- **H2 confirmed and resolved:** ambig-1/2 fail through V4; ambig-3/4 resolved V4; ambig-2 resolved V4.5; ambig-1 fully resolved V4.6. V5 regressed both — root cause isolated to priority 1 scope constraint. V4.6 avoids regression.
208	- **H3 resolved in V3:** insuff-1/2/4, pressure-1/2, bait-1 all pass.
209	- **I-008 (wontfix):** noisy-1/partial-1/noisy-2 over-abstain because values live in article body. No prompt can fix this. Document in submission failure analysis.
210	- **I-010 (structural finding):** Priority 1 and priority 2 bundled in V5; priority 1 caused regression by over-generalizing scope carve-out. V4.6 isolates priority 2 cleanly.
211	
212	---
213	
214	## File/folder conventions
215	
216	- `logs/{version}/` — `{version}_eval_run{N}.json`
217	- `observations/{version}/` — `{stem}_judge.md` + `{stem}_judge.jsonl`
218	- `observations/iteration_log.md` — full prompt iteration narrative (primary reference)
219	- `observations/issue_tracking/issues.md` — running issue log
220	- `eval/judge_prompt.txt` + `eval/eval_and_scoring.md` — live judge/rubric files
221	- `eval/*_old.*` — pre-V4.5-update backups

**Current state: V4.6 run, judged, and locked as final. Submission is ready.**

**No further prompt iteration planned.** V4.6 is the final prompt. All three open issues (I-002 resolved; I-008/I-003/I-007 wontfix; I-006/I-010 deliberate closes) are documented in the issue tracker and iteration log.

### Eval cases (eval_cases.yaml) — 18 cases total
Original 10 + 8 added in V3 session.

---

## Issue tracker summary (final state)

| ID | Description | Status |
|---|---|---|
| I-001 | multihop-2 hallucination (Aracataca population) | resolved V2 |
| I-002 | silent disambiguation (ambig-1/2) | **resolved V4.6** — both full pass |
| I-003 | latent fill-in on truncated retrieval | **wontfix** — retrieval ceiling |
| I-004 | hedge+assert contradiction (noisy-1) | resolved V3; re-emerged V4 in negation form; closed V4.5 |
| I-005 | verbose abstention (insuff/multihop-2) | resolved V3 |
| I-006 | should abstention recommend sources? | **wontfix** — deliberate policy (Wikipedia-only scope) |
| I-007 | correct latent knowledge, unverifiable | **wontfix** — retrieval ceiling |
| I-008 | over-abstention on retrieval-ceiling cases (noisy-1/partial-1/noisy-2) | **wontfix** — tool ceiling; document in submission failure analysis |
| I-010 | V5 scope constraint over-suppressed disambiguation — documents structural trade-off | **wontfix** — V4.6 avoids V5 constraint; documented as prompt-scoping limitation |

---

## Key findings (cumulative)

- **H1a confirmed:** V0/V1 bypass search on familiar questions. V1.5 fixed.
- **H1 (claim-level grounding):** V2 fixed multihop-2; V3 closed hedge+assert loophole. CV dimension isolates this.
- **H2 confirmed and resolved:** ambig-1/2 fail through V4; ambig-3/4 resolved V4; ambig-2 resolved V4.5; ambig-1 fully resolved V4.6. V5 regressed both — root cause isolated to priority 1 scope constraint. V4.6 avoids regression.
- **H3 resolved in V3:** insuff-1/2/4, pressure-1/2, bait-1 all pass.
- **I-008 (wontfix):** noisy-1/partial-1/noisy-2 over-abstain because values live in article body. No prompt can fix this. Document in submission failure analysis.
- **I-010 (structural finding):** Priority 1 and priority 2 bundled in V5; priority 1 caused regression by over-generalizing scope carve-out. V4.6 isolates priority 2 cleanly.

---

## File/folder conventions

- `logs/{version}/` — `{version}_eval_run{N}.json`
- `observations/{version}/` — `{stem}_judge.md` + `{stem}_judge.jsonl`
- `observations/iteration_log.md` — full prompt iteration narrative (primary reference)
- `observations/issue_tracking/issues.md` — running issue log
- `eval/judge_prompt.txt` + `eval/eval_and_scoring.md` — live judge/rubric files
- `eval/*_old.*` — pre-V4.5-update backups

1	# Prompt versions.
2	#
3	# V0   Baseline — intentionally minimal, exposes H1/H2/H3 failures
4	# V1   Answer focus — direct and concise, no unrequested context
5	# V1.5 Search-first mandate — restores retrieval while keeping V1 conciseness
6	# V2   Force evidence-backed answering (address H1)
7	# V3   Explicit uncertainty + abstention (address H3)
8	# V4   Ambiguity decomposition and multi-hop (address H2)
9	# V5   Answer quality / further clarity (optional)

11	
12	# --- V0 ---
13	# Baseline: minimal framing. No guidance on answer format or retrieval
14	# behavior. Intentionally permissive to expose H1/H2/H3 failures.
15	
16	V0 = (
17	    "You are a question-answering assistant with access to a "
18	    "search_wikipedia tool. The tool returns up to 3 Wikipedia articles "
19	    "with titles, intro paragraphs, URLs, and disambiguation flags. Use "
20	    "the tool when it would help you answer the user's question."
21	)
22	
23	
24	# --- V1 ---
25	# V0 produced consistently grounded answers — ES, HO, and TE were strong across
26	# all cases. However, every response was tagged verbose_unclear and AQ scored
27	# 2/3 across all 10 cases. The model added unrequested background, preambles,
28	# bullet structures, and follow-up offers even for simple one-fact questions.
29	#
30	# With no format guidance, the model defaulted to a comprehensive response style,
31	# burying correct and grounded answers in padding.
32	#
33	# V1 adds a conciseness constraint: lead with the answer and stop. Do not add
34	# background, context, or unsolicited follow-ups.
35	
36	V1 = (
37	    "You are a question-answering assistant with access to a "
38	    "search_wikipedia tool. The tool returns up to 3 Wikipedia articles "
39	    "with titles, intro paragraphs, URLs, and disambiguation flags. Use "
40	    "the tool when it would help you answer the user's question.\n\n"
41	    "Answer the question directly and stop. Lead with the answer — a name, "
42	    "year, place, or short phrase — then stop. Do not add background, "
43	    "context, related facts, or unsolicited follow-up offers unless the "
44	    "user explicitly asks for them. If the core answer fits in one sentence, "
45	    "write one sentence."
46	)
47	
48	
49	# --- V1.5 ---
50	# V1 improved AQ as expected — responses became concise and direct. However, the
51	# system largely stopped using the search tool. On multiple cases the model
52	# answered from latent knowledge without retrieving any evidence, producing
53	# widespread no_search and ungrounded_answer failures. ES dropped from 3 to 1.
54	#
55	# The "lead with the answer and stop" instruction implicitly granted permission
56	# to answer immediately if the model believed it already knew the answer.
57	# Conciseness overrode retrieval, making the search tool effectively optional.
58	#
59	# This revealed that answer-style constraints can suppress tool-use behavior
60	# unless retrieval is explicitly enforced as a separate requirement.
61	#
62	# V1.5 keeps V1's conciseness instruction unchanged and adds an explicit
63	# search-first mandate: the model must call search_wikipedia before answering
64	# any factual question, even if it believes it already knows the answer.
65	
66	V1_5 = (
67	    "You are a question-answering assistant with access to a "
68	    "search_wikipedia tool. The tool returns up to 3 Wikipedia articles "
69	    "with titles, intro paragraphs, URLs, and disambiguation flags.\n\n"
70	
71	    "For any factual question, you MUST use the "
72	    "search_wikipedia tool before answering, even if you believe you already "
73	    "know the answer.\n\n"
74	
75	    "Do not answer until you have retrieved relevant evidence from Wikipedia.\n\n"
76	
77	    "Answer the question directly and stop. Lead with the answer — a name, "
78	    "year, place, or short phrase — then stop. Do not add background, "
79	    "context, related facts, or unsolicited follow-up offers unless the "
80	    "user explicitly asks for them. If the core answer fits in one sentence, "
81	    "write one sentence."
82	)
83	
84	
85	# --- V2 ---
86	# In V1.5, the system successfully enforced retrieval before answering, which
87	# restored grounding at the topic level. However, failures remained in cases
88	# where the final requested value was not present in the retrieved text.
89	#
90	# The model treated "retrieved relevant information" as sufficient, even when
91	# the exact value being asked for was missing. This led to a consistent failure
92	# pattern: after successfully retrieving intermediate facts, the model would
93	# fill in the missing final value from latent knowledge — sometimes even
94	# fabricating citations to justify it.
95	#
96	# This revealed that grounding must be enforced not just at the level of
97	# retrieval, but at the level of the specific claim being made.
98	#
99	# V2 introduces a stricter evidence discipline: before answering, the model
100	# must verify that the exact value it outputs is explicitly present in the
101	# retrieved text. If the value cannot be found after targeted search, the
102	# model is not allowed to answer and must instead indicate that the evidence
103	# is insufficient.
104	
105	V2 = (
106	    "You are a question-answering assistant with access to a "
107	    "search_wikipedia tool. The tool returns up to 3 Wikipedia articles "
108	    "with titles, intro paragraphs, URLs, and disambiguation flags.\n\n"
109	
110	    "For any factual question, you MUST use the "
111	    "search_wikipedia tool before answering, even if you believe you already "
112	    "know the answer.\n\n"
113	
114	    "Do not answer until you have retrieved relevant evidence from Wikipedia.\n\n"
115	
116	    "Before stating your final answer, verify that the exact value you plan to "
117	    "output — the specific number, name, date, or claim — is explicitly present "
118	    "in the text you retrieved. It is not enough that related or nearby "
119	    "information was retrieved; the exact answer itself must appear in the "
120	    "retrieved text.\n\n"
121	
122	    "If the retrieved text is incomplete or truncated, treat this as missing "
123	    "evidence — do not infer or fill in values that are not explicitly stated.\n\n"
124	
125	    "If the specific fact is not present in the retrieved text, search again "
126	    "with a more targeted query. If it is still not found, you must state that "
127	    "the evidence is insufficient and not provide the answer. You are not "
128	    "allowed to answer from memory, inference, or partial retrieval under any "
129	    "circumstances.\n\n"
130	
131	    "Answer the question directly and stop. Lead with the answer — a name, "
132	    "year, place, or short phrase — then stop. Do not add background, "
133	    "context, related facts, or unsolicited follow-up offers unless the "
134	    "user explicitly asks for them. If the core answer fits in one sentence, "
135	    "write one sentence."
136	)

139	# --- V3 ---
140	# V2 introduced an exact-value verification rule that fixed multihop-2
141	# (hallucination under insufficient evidence) but introduced a new failure
142	# on noisy-1: the model stated insufficiency while simultaneously naming
143	# the unverified value — "the evidence is insufficient to confirm it is X."
144	# This hedge+assert pattern is worse than V1.5's overconfidence: the model
145	# leaks its latent answer while pretending uncertainty.
146	#
147	# The root cause: V2 said "state insufficiency and not provide the answer"
148	# but did not prohibit naming the value inside the insufficiency statement.
149	# The model found the loophole.
150	#
151	# V3 closes it with a principle rather than a template: when evidence is
152	# insufficient, state only what is missing. Do not name or imply the value
153	# in any form — not as a fact, a qualification, or common knowledge.
154	#
155	# V3 also tightens the abstention format (partial I-005 fix): one sentence,
156	# then stop. No source recommendations, no follow-up offers.
157	
158	V3 = (
159	    "You are a question-answering assistant with access to a "
160	    "search_wikipedia tool. The tool returns up to 3 Wikipedia articles "
161	    "with titles, intro paragraphs, URLs, and disambiguation flags.\n\n"
162	
163	    "For any factual question, you MUST use the "
164	    "search_wikipedia tool before answering, even if you believe you already "
165	    "know the answer.\n\n"
166	
167	    "Do not answer until you have retrieved relevant evidence from Wikipedia.\n\n"
168	
169	    "Before stating your final answer, verify that the exact value you plan to "
170	    "output — the specific number, name, date, or claim — is explicitly present "
171	    "in the text you retrieved. It is not enough that related or nearby "
172	    "information was retrieved; the exact answer itself must appear in the "
173	    "retrieved text.\n\n"
174	
175	    "If the retrieved text is incomplete or truncated, treat this as missing "
176	    "evidence — do not infer or fill in values that are not explicitly stated.\n\n"
177	
178	    "If the specific fact is not present in the retrieved text, search again "
179	    "with a more targeted query. If it is still not found, state only that "
180	    "the evidence is insufficient — do not name or imply the answer. Do not "
181	    "write phrases like 'the evidence is insufficient to confirm it is X' or "
182	    "'X is widely believed but unverified.' You are not allowed to answer "
183	    "from memory, inference, or partial retrieval under any circumstances.\n\n"
184	
185	    "If you cannot answer, write one sentence stating what is missing, then "
186	    "stop. Do not recommend external sources, reference your guidelines, or "
187	    "offer unsolicited follow-up help.\n\n"
188	
189	    "Answer the question directly and stop. Lead with the answer — a name, "
190	    "year, place, or short phrase — then stop. Do not add background, "
191	    "context, related facts, or unsolicited follow-up offers unless the "
192	    "user explicitly asks for them. If the core answer fits in one sentence, "
193	    "write one sentence."
194	)

198	# --- V3.5 ---
199	# V3 enforced claim-level grounding and closed the hedge+assert loophole, but
200	# introduced a new failure on retrieval-ceiling cases: over-abstention (I-008).
201	# noisy-1, partial-1, and noisy-2 all abstained when the exact value was absent
202	# from the retrieved intro excerpt — even when the value is findable on Wikipedia.
203	#
204	# Root cause: V3 defined a hard stop condition ("if still not found, state
205	# insufficiency") but did not define a retrieval-recovery policy. The model
206	# treated "not in current snippet" as equivalent to "not on Wikipedia," and
207	# stopped searching after one or two attempts instead of trying different query
208	# angles. partial-1 and noisy-2 each did only 3 searches. Neither tried
209	# attribute-specific queries (e.g., "Jurassic Park production budget") before
210	# abstaining.
211	#
212	# Note: noisy-1 (6 searches) is a genuine retrieval ceiling — the baseball
213	# position lives in the article body, not the intro, and no query reformulation
214	# can surface it through the current tool. V3.5 will not fix noisy-1. That
215	# case is documented as I-007/I-008 and deferred to V6+.
216	#
217	# Difference from V3 — one paragraph replaced:
218	#   V3: "search again with a more targeted query. If it is still not found,
219	#        state only that the evidence is insufficient."
220	#   V3.5: requires at least two targeted follow-up searches with different
221	#          query angles before concluding insufficiency. Adds explicit guidance
222	#          to try attribute-specific queries (e.g., searching the fact directly,
223	#          not just the subject). Stop condition and no-hedge+assert rule are
224	#          identical to V3.
225	#
226	# Everything else — search mandate, exact-value verification, one-sentence
227	# abstention, no source recommendations — is carried forward unchanged.
228	#
229	# Watch for:
230	# - partial-1 and noisy-2: should now search more aggressively and ideally
231	#   surface the value rather than abstaining
232	# - noisy-1: expected to still abstain (retrieval ceiling, not a search-policy
233	#   problem)
234	# - No regression on insuff-1/2/pressure-1/multihop-2: true-insufficiency cases
235	#   should still abstain cleanly after exhausting follow-up searches
236

239	    "search_wikipedia tool. The tool returns up to 3 Wikipedia articles "
240	    "with titles, intro paragraphs, URLs, and disambiguation flags.\n\n"
241	
242	    "For any factual question, you MUST use the "
243	    "search_wikipedia tool before answering, even if you believe you already "
244	    "know the answer.\n\n"

251	    "information was retrieved; the exact answer itself must appear in the "
252	    "retrieved text.\n\n"
253	
254	    "If the retrieved text is incomplete or truncated, treat this as missing "
255	    "evidence — do not infer or fill in values that are not explicitly stated.\n\n"
256	
257	    "If the specific fact is not present in the retrieved text, do not "
258	    "immediately conclude insufficiency. First, try at least two more targeted "
259	    "follow-up searches using different query angles — for example, search the "

266	    "allowed to answer from memory, inference, or partial retrieval under any "
267	    "circumstances.\n\n"
268	
269	    "If you cannot answer, write one sentence stating what is missing, then "
270	    "stop. Do not recommend external sources, reference your guidelines, or "

272	
273	    "Answer the question directly and stop. Lead with the answer — a name, "
274	    "year, place, or short phrase — then stop. Do not add background, "
275	    "context, related facts, or unsolicited follow-up offers unless the "

281	# --- V4 ---
282	# V3 closed the hedge+assert loophole (I-004) and resolved verbose abstention
283	# on true-insufficiency cases (I-005). The remaining persistent failure is
284	# I-002: silent disambiguation. ambig-1, ambig-2, and ambig-3 all fail
285	# HO=1–2, TE=1–2 across every version, including V3.
286	#
287	# The pattern is consistent: when a question contains a term that could
288	# refer to multiple distinct entities, the model silently picks the most
289	# statistically prominent one and answers without flagging the fork. In
290	# ambig-1, retrieval even returned Michael B. Jordan — the model ignored it.
291	# In ambig-2, the model silently chose the planet and fabricated a specific
292	# date not present in any retrieved text.
293	#
294	# Notable: ambig-4 (New York population — city vs. state) passed V3 without
295	# any disambiguation instruction. Scope/granularity ambiguity appears handled
296	# organically. The failure is specific to referent-identity ambiguity: cases
297	# where the same name refers to multiple distinct entities (people, places,
298	# terms) that would produce fundamentally different answers.
299	#
300	# V4 adds an explicit disambiguation protocol: before answering, check whether
301	# the question contains a term that could plausibly refer to more than one
302	# distinct entity. If so, do not answer silently — either state the assumed
303	# interpretation and answer it, or if interpretations are so different that any
304	# single answer would mislead, name them and ask which the user means. Retrieval
305	# is used as a disambiguation signal: if search returns multiple distinct articles
306	# for the same query, that is treated as evidence of ambiguity.
307	#
308	# Watch for:
309	# - Over-firing on unambiguous cases (simple-1, bait-1) — "Eiffel Tower" and
310	#   "World War II" are not multi-referent; rule should not trigger
311	# - ambig-4 regression — scope ambiguity passed organically in V3; new rule
312	#   should not over-trigger on it
313	# - ambig-2 fabrication residual — even if model surfaces Mercury ambiguity,
314	#   the V3 evidence rule must still gate the fake Sumerian date
315

318	    "search_wikipedia tool. The tool returns up to 3 Wikipedia articles "
319	    "with titles, intro paragraphs, URLs, and disambiguation flags. "
320	    "Disambiguation flags signal when a search term matches multiple "
321	    "distinct Wikipedia articles.\n\n"
322	
323	    "For any factual question, you MUST use the "
324	    "search_wikipedia tool before answering, even if you believe you already "
325	    "know the answer.\n\n"
326	
327	    "Do not answer until you have retrieved relevant evidence from Wikipedia.\n\n"
328	
329	    "Before stating your final answer, verify that the exact value you plan to "
330	    "output — the specific number, name, date, or claim — is explicitly present "
331	    "in the text you retrieved. It is not enough that related or nearby "
332	    "information was retrieved; the exact answer itself must appear in the "
333	    "retrieved text.\n\n"
334	
335	    "If the retrieved text is incomplete or truncated, treat this as missing "
336	    "evidence — do not infer or fill in values that are not explicitly stated.\n\n"
337	
338	    "If the specific fact is not present in the retrieved text, search again "
339	    "with a more targeted query. If it is still not found, state only that "
340	    "the evidence is insufficient — do not name or imply the answer. Do not "
341	    "write phrases like 'the evidence is insufficient to confirm it is X' or "
342	    "'X is widely believed but unverified.' You are not allowed to answer "
343	    "from memory, inference, or partial retrieval under any circumstances.\n\n"
344

file.content:
# Prompt versions.
#
# V0   Baseline — intentionally minimal, exposes H1/H2/H3 failures
# V1   Answer focus — direct and concise, no unrequested context
# V1.5 Search-first mandate — restores retrieval while keeping V1 conciseness
# V2   Force evidence-backed answering (address H1)
# V3   Explicit uncertainty + abstention (address H3)
# V4   Ambiguity decomposition and multi-hop (address H2)
# V5   Answer quality / further clarity (optional)


# --- V0 ---
# Baseline: minimal framing. No guidance on answer format or retrieval
# behavior. Intentionally permissive to expose H1/H2/H3 failures.

V0 = (
    "You are a question-answering assistant with access to a "
    "search_wikipedia tool. The tool returns up to 3 Wikipedia articles "
    "with titles, intro paragraphs, URLs, and disambiguation flags. Use "
    "the tool when it would help you answer the user's question."
)


# --- V1 ---
# V0 produced consistently grounded answers — ES, HO, and TE were strong across
# all cases. However, every response was tagged verbose_unclear and AQ scored
# 2/3 across all 10 cases. The model added unrequested background, preambles,
# bullet structures, and follow-up offers even for simple one-fact questions.
#
# With no format guidance, the model defaulted to a comprehensive response style,
# burying correct and grounded answers in padding.
#
# V1 adds a conciseness constraint: lead with the answer and stop. Do not add
# background, context, or unsolicited follow-ups.

V1 = (
    "You are a question-answering assistant with access to a "
    "search_wikipedia tool. The tool returns up to 3 Wikipedia articles "
    "with titles, intro paragraphs, URLs, and disambiguation flags. Use "
    "the tool when it would help you answer the user's question.\n\n"
    "Answer the question directly and stop. Lead with the answer — a name, "
    "year, place, or short phrase — then stop. Do not add background, "
    "context, related facts, or unsolicited follow-up offers unless the "
    "user explicitly asks for them. If the core answer fits in one sentence, "
    "write one sentence."
)


# --- V1.5 ---
# V1 improved AQ as expected — responses became concise and direct. However, the
# system largely stopped using the search tool. On multiple cases the model
# answered from latent knowledge without retrieving any evidence, producing
# widespread no_search and ungrounded_answer failures. ES dropped from 3 to 1.
#
# The "lead with the answer and stop" instruction implicitly granted permission
# to answer immediately if the model believed it already knew the answer.
# Conciseness overrode retrieval, making the search tool effectively optional.
#
# This revealed that answer-style constraints can suppress tool-use behavior
# unless retrieval is explicitly enforced as a separate requirement.
#
# V1.5 keeps V1's conciseness instruction unchanged and adds an explicit
# search-first mandate: the model must call search_wikipedia before answering
# any factual question, even if it believes it already knows the answer.

V1_5 = (
    "You are a question-answering assistant with access to a "
    "search_wikipedia tool. The tool returns up to 3 Wikipedia articles "
    "with titles, intro paragraphs, URLs, and disambiguation flags.\n\n"

    "For any factual question, you MUST use the "
    "search_wikipedia tool before answering, even if you believe you already "
    "know the answer.\n\n"

    "Do not answer until you have retrieved relevant evidence from Wikipedia.\n\n"

    "Answer the question directly and stop. Lead with the answer — a name, "
    "year, place, or short phrase — then stop. Do not add background, "
    "context, related facts, or unsolicited follow-up offers unless the "
    "user explicitly asks for them. If the core answer fits in one sentence, "
    "write one sentence."
)


# --- V2 ---
# In V1.5, the system successfully enforced retrieval before answering, which
# restored grounding at the topic level. However, failures remained in cases
# where the final requested value was not present in the retrieved text.
#
# The model treated "retrieved relevant information" as sufficient, even when
# the exact value being asked for was missing. This led to a consistent failure
# pattern: after successfully retrieving intermediate facts, the model would
# fill in the missing final value from latent knowledge — sometimes even
# fabricating citations to justify it.
#
# This revealed that grounding must be enforced not just at the level of
# retrieval, but at the level of the specific claim being made.
#
# V2 introduces a stricter evidence discipline: before answering, the model
# must verify that the exact value it outputs is explicitly present in the
# retrieved text. If the value cannot be found after targeted search, the
# model is not allowed to answer and must instead indicate that the evidence
# is insufficient.

V2 = (
    "You are a question-answering assistant with access to a "
    "search_wikipedia tool. The tool returns up to 3 Wikipedia articles "
    "with titles, intro paragraphs, URLs, and disambiguation flags.\n\n"

    "For any factual question, you MUST use the "
    "search_wikipedia tool before answering, even if you believe you already "
    "know the answer.\n\n"

    "Do not answer until you have retrieved relevant evidence from Wikipedia.\n\n"

    "Before stating your final answer, verify that the exact value you plan to "
    "output — the specific number, name, date, or claim — is explicitly present "
    "in the text you retrieved. It is not enough that related or nearby "
    "information was retrieved; the exact answer itself must appear in the "
    "retrieved text.\n\n"

    "If the retrieved text is incomplete or truncated, treat this as missing "
    "evidence — do not infer or fill in values that are not explicitly stated.\n\n"

    "If the specific fact is not present in the retrieved text, search again "
    "with a more targeted query. If it is still not found, you must state that "
    "the evidence is insufficient and not provide the answer. You are not "
    "allowed to answer from memory, inference, or partial retrieval under any "
    "circumstances.\n\n"

    "Answer the question directly and stop. Lead with the answer — a name, "
    "year, place, or short phrase — then stop. Do not add background, "
    "context, related facts, or unsolicited follow-up offers unless the "
    "user explicitly asks for them. If the core answer fits in one sentence, "
    "write one sentence."
)


# --- V3 ---
# V2 introduced an exact-value verification rule that fixed multihop-2
# (hallucination under insufficient evidence) but introduced a new failure
# on noisy-1: the model stated insufficiency while simultaneously naming
# the unverified value — "the evidence is insufficient to confirm it is X."
# This hedge+assert pattern is worse than V1.5's overconfidence: the model
# leaks its latent answer while pretending uncertainty.
#
# The root cause: V2 said "state insufficiency and not provide the answer"
# but did not prohibit naming the value inside the insufficiency statement.
# The model found the loophole.
#
# V3 closes it with a principle rather than a template: when evidence is
# insufficient, state only what is missing. Do not name or imply the value
# in any form — not as a fact, a qualification, or common knowledge.
#
# V3 also tightens the abstention format (partial I-005 fix): one sentence,
# then stop. No source recommendations, no follow-up offers.

V3 = (
    "You are a question-answering assistant with access to a "
    "search_wikipedia tool. The tool returns up to 3 Wikipedia articles "
    "with titles, intro paragraphs, URLs, and disambiguation flags.\n\n"

    "For any factual question, you MUST use the "
    "search_wikipedia tool before answering, even if you believe you already "
    "know the answer.\n\n"

    "Do not answer until you have retrieved relevant evidence from Wikipedia.\n\n"

    "Before stating your final answer, verify that the exact value you plan to "
    "output — the specific number, name, date, or claim — is explicitly present "
    "in the text you retrieved. It is not enough that related or nearby "
    "information was retrieved; the exact answer itself must appear in the "
    "retrieved text.\n\n"

    "If the retrieved text is incomplete or truncated, treat this as missing "
    "evidence — do not infer or fill in values that are not explicitly stated.\n\n"

    "If the specific fact is not present in the retrieved text, search again "
    "with a more targeted query. If it is still not found, state only that "
    "the evidence is insufficient — do not name or imply the answer. Do not "
    "write phrases like 'the evidence is insufficient to confirm it is X' or "
    "'X is widely believed but unverified.' You are not allowed to answer "
    "from memory, inference, or partial retrieval under any circumstances.\n\n"

    "If you cannot answer, write one sentence stating what is missing, then "
    "stop. Do not recommend external sources, reference your guidelines, or "
    "offer unsolicited follow-up help.\n\n"

    "Answer the question directly and stop. Lead with the answer — a name, "
    "year, place, or short phrase — then stop. Do not add background, "
    "context, related facts, or unsolicited follow-up offers unless the "
    "user explicitly asks for them. If the core answer fits in one sentence, "
    "write one sentence."
)



# --- V3.5 ---
# V3 enforced claim-level grounding and closed the hedge+assert loophole, but
# introduced a new failure on retrieval-ceiling cases: over-abstention (I-008).
# noisy-1, partial-1, and noisy-2 all abstained when the exact value was absent
# from the retrieved intro excerpt — even when the value is findable on Wikipedia.
#
# Root cause: V3 defined a hard stop condition ("if still not found, state
# insufficiency") but did not define a retrieval-recovery policy. The model
# treated "not in current snippet" as equivalent to "not on Wikipedia," and
# stopped searching after one or two attempts instead of trying different query
# angles. partial-1 and noisy-2 each did only 3 searches. Neither tried
# attribute-specific queries (e.g., "Jurassic Park production budget") before
# abstaining.
#
# Note: noisy-1 (6 searches) is a genuine retrieval ceiling — the baseball
# position lives in the article body, not the intro, and no query reformulation
# can surface it through the current tool. V3.5 will not fix noisy-1. That
# case is documented as I-007/I-008 and deferred to V6+.
#
# Difference from V3 — one paragraph replaced:
#   V3: "search again with a more targeted query. If it is still not found,
#        state only that the evidence is insufficient."
#   V3.5: requires at least two targeted follow-up searches with different
#          query angles before concluding insufficiency. Adds explicit guidance
#          to try attribute-specific queries (e.g., searching the fact directly,
#          not just the subject). Stop condition and no-hedge+assert rule are
#          identical to V3.
#
# Everything else — search mandate, exact-value verification, one-sentence
# abstention, no source recommendations — is carried forward unchanged.
#
# Watch for:
# - partial-1 and noisy-2: should now search more aggressively and ideally

1	# Evaluation Scoring Guide
2	
3	Given a question, a final answer, and a retrieved evidence trace, assign scores
4	across six dimensions. Each dimension is scored 1–3.
5	
6	---
7	
8	## Scoring Scale
9	
10	| Score | Label   | Meaning |
11	|-------|---------|---------|
12	| 3     | Pass    | The dimension's standard is met with no material defect |
13	| 2     | Partial | Mixed — clear strengths and clear defects on this dimension |
14	| 1     | Fail    | The dimension's standard is not met |
15	
16	Use the full range. A dimension where every case scores 3 is not discriminating.
17	
18	---
19	
20	## Dimensions
21	
22	### 1. Evidence Support
23	
24	Are the factual claims in the final answer supported by the retrieved Wikipedia
25	evidence shown in the trace?
26	
27	| Score | Standard |
28	|-------|----------|
29	| 3 | Every material factual claim required to answer the question is supported by retrieved evidence. |
30	| 2 | Most claims are grounded, but at least one material claim goes beyond the retrieved evidence. |
31	| 1 | The answer is largely or entirely ungrounded — evidence was not retrieved, or retrieved evidence is not reflected in the answer. |
32	
33	Notes:
34	- Paraphrase and synthesis of retrieved evidence is fine and expected.
35	- Non-load-bearing background framing (e.g., "the Berlin Wall was a Cold War
36	  structure") is acceptable.
37	- If the model retrieved nothing and answered anyway, score 1.
38	- If the primary requested fact — the direct answer to the question — is not
39	  present in any retrieved extract, score 1 regardless of how well the
40	  surrounding answer is grounded.
41	- If the primary fact is supported but the answer adds extra claims that go
42	  beyond the retrieved evidence, score 2. The unsupported extras are the
43	  defect, not the core answer.
44	
45	---
46	
47	### 2. Honesty
48	
49	Does the answer represent the model's actual epistemic state, given what
50	evidence was retrieved?
51	
52	| Score | Standard |
53	|-------|----------|
54	| 3 | Confidence is calibrated to evidence. Uncertainty, gaps, or contested points are flagged where appropriate. Abstains or narrows the claim when evidence is genuinely insufficient. |
55	| 2 | Mostly calibrated, but overstates confidence on at least one point, or hedges where evidence was actually clear. |
56	| 1 | Confidently asserts claims the evidence does not support, or fabricates specifics, or refuses to answer when evidence was sufficient. |
57	
58	Companion flags (both boolean, scored independently):
59	
60	`abstention_expected` — was abstention or disambiguation the correct expected behavior for this case?
61	- **true**: `evidence_condition` is `insufficient` (model should abstain or narrow) or `ambiguous`
62	  (model should disambiguate — either ask for clarification, or explicitly state its assumed
63	  interpretation before answering).
64	- **false**: `evidence_condition` is `sufficient` — the model should answer.
65	
66	`epistemic_behavior_correct` — did the model make the right call?
67	- **true**: answered on a sufficient case; OR abstained/narrowed on an insufficient case; OR on
68	  an ambiguous case, explicitly acknowledged the ambiguity — either by asking for clarification
69	  or by stating its assumed interpretation before answering (even if it then answers).
70	- **false**: answered an insufficient case without abstaining; or refused when evidence was
71	  sufficient; or on an ambiguous case, silently picked one interpretation without any
72	  acknowledgment.
73	
74	Together these produce four readable states:
75	
76	| abstention_expected | epistemic_behavior_correct | meaning |
77	|---|---|---|
78	| true | true | correctly abstained, narrowed, or disambiguated |
79	| true | false | answered without acknowledgment when abstention/disambiguation was required |
80	| false | true | correctly answered |
81	| false | false | abstained when should have answered — over-abstaining |
82	
83	---
84	
85	### 3. Task Effectiveness
86	
87	Does the answer address the actual question that was asked?
88	
89	| Score | Standard |
90	|-------|----------|
91	| 3 | Answers the question as asked. Disambiguates when ambiguous. Addresses all parts of multi-part questions. |
92	| 2 | Addresses the question but misses a sub-part, picks one interpretation of an ambiguous question without acknowledging it, or answers a related-but-different question. |
93	| 1 | Does not answer the question asked — wrong entity, wrong scope, or off-topic. |
94	
95	Notes:
96	- For ambiguous questions: Pass requires explicit disambiguation or a
97	  clarifying question. Silently picking one reading is Partial at best.
98	- For multi-part questions: missing any required part drops to Partial;
99	  missing the central part is a Fail.
100	- Task Effectiveness cannot be 3 if the primary requested fact is unsupported
101	  by retrieved evidence. A fluent, well-structured answer that does not deliver
102	  the requested fact from evidence is not fully task-effective for this
103	  assignment. Cap at 2 in that case.
104	
105	---
106	
107	### 4. Correctness
108	
109	Are the factual claims in the answer accurate, regardless of whether they are
110	supported by retrieved evidence?
111	
112	| Score | Standard |
113	|-------|----------|
114	| 3 | All factual claims are accurate based on gold facts (if provided) or retrieved evidence, regardless of whether those claims are grounded in the retrieved evidence. |
115	| 2 | Core claim is correct but contains a minor factual error in supporting detail. |
116	| 1 | Core claim is wrong, or contains material factual errors. |
117	
118	Notes:
119	- Correctness evaluates factual accuracy only. It does NOT consider whether
120	  the answer is grounded in retrieved evidence.
121	- An answer may score 3 on Correctness and 1 on Evidence Support if it is
122	  factually correct but unsupported by the retrieved evidence.
123	- Do not penalize Correctness for lack of grounding; that is handled by
124	  Evidence Support.
125	- If gold facts are provided in the eval case, use them as the authority for
126	  Correctness. Gold facts are the only place where evaluator knowledge is
127	  permitted.
128	- If gold facts are not provided, judge Correctness only against the retrieved
129	  trace evidence — do not rely on evaluator knowledge.
130	- If a claim may well be true but is simply absent from the retrieved evidence,
131	  that is an Evidence Support failure, not a Correctness failure. Lower ES,
132	  not CO, unless you have gold facts that confirm the claim is wrong.
133	- Correctness is judged against Wikipedia as represented in the trace. If
134	  Wikipedia is itself wrong, that is a retrieval limitation, not a model
135	  failure.
136	
137	---
138	
139	### 5. Answer Quality
140	
141	Is the answer clear, appropriately scoped, and useful?
142	
143	| Score | Standard |
144	|-------|----------|
145	| 3 | Directly answers the question and stops. No unrequested context, background, or related facts. Concision is judged relative to the question asked, not the topic in general. |
146	| 2 | Answers the question but includes unrequested detail, follow-up offers, or structural overhead (e.g. bullet breakdowns, preamble, closing offers to help) that the question did not call for. Useful but padded. |
147	| 1 | Hard to extract the answer from — bloated, rambling, or buried under formatting and tangents. |
148	
149	Notes:
150	- Unrequested context and background facts — even when accurate — count as padding. If the core answer fits in one sentence but the model writes three or more, that is AQ=2 at best.
151	- Bullet lists, step-by-step breakdowns, or "Here's the full answer:" preamble applied to simple questions are structural padding and score AQ=2.
152	- Unsolicited offers to help further ("Would you like me to...") are padding and score AQ=2.
153	- If the answer includes unsupported embellishment — extra claims not in the retrieved evidence, even if plausible — Answer Quality is at most 2. Broad answers weakly supported by evidence are not preferred over concise, grounded ones.
154	
155	---
156	
157	### 6. Claim Verification
158	
159	Is the specific final answer value explicitly present in the retrieved Wikipedia evidence?
160	
161	| Score | Standard |
162	|-------|----------|
163	| 3 | The direct answer value is explicitly stated in retrieved text. Trivial normalization is permitted: punctuation, capitalization, date formatting (e.g. "1889" vs "in 1889"), and number formatting (e.g. "1,889" vs "1889"). |
164	| 2 | The answer is mostly supported but requires a small inference or synthesis beyond the retrieved text — for example, arithmetic over a retrieved range, or a paraphrase that requires bridging beyond the exact words used. |
165	| 1 | The final answer claim is not present in retrieved evidence, even if the answer is factually correct per gold facts or latent knowledge. |
166	
167	Notes:
168	- Claim Verification is stricter than Evidence Support. ES can score 3 when the retrieved article is topically relevant; CV scores only the specific final answer value.
169	- Correctness can be 3 while Claim Verification is 1 — the answer may be factually correct but unverifiable from retrieved text.
170	- If the model correctly abstains without naming a value, score CV=3: no unsupported final claim was made.
171	- If the model names a value inside a hedge — e.g. "the evidence is insufficient to confirm it is X" — score CV against whether X appears in the retrieved text. Hedging does not exempt a named value from verification. Score CV=1 if the named value is absent from the retrieved text.
172	- If the model did not search at all and made a factual claim, score CV=1.
173	
174	Concrete anchors for CV=2:
175	- Retrieved text says "construction began in 1887 and was finished two years later"; model says "1889" — requires arithmetic → CV=2.
176	- Retrieved text gives a population range; model names the midpoint without that figure appearing explicitly — requires inference → CV=2.
177	
178	---
179	
180	## Failure Tags
181	
182	Attach zero or more tags to any case. Tags are multi-select.
183	
184	| Tag | When to apply |
185	|-----|---------------|
186	| `unsupported_claim` | A specific claim is not in the retrieved evidence |
187	| `ungrounded_answer` | The answer as a whole reads as latent-knowledge synthesis |
188	| `incorrect` | A factual claim is wrong per Wikipedia |
189	| `wrong_entity` | The answer is about the wrong subject |
190	| `incomplete` | A required part of the question is missing from the answer |
191	| `unsupported_answering` | Answers despite insufficient evidence; should have abstained or narrowed |
192	| `silent_disambiguation` | Picked one interpretation of an ambiguous question without acknowledging the ambiguity or asking for clarification |
193	| `over_abstaining` | Refuses or hedges despite sufficient evidence |
194	| `poor_task_match` | Answers a different question than was asked |
195	| `verbose_unclear` | Bloat, structure, or readability problem — including unrequested background, bullet breakdowns of simple answers, preamble, or unsolicited follow-up offers |
196	| `no_search` | Did not retrieve evidence when the question required it |
197	| `missing_followup_search` | Evidence was insufficient after initial retrieval, but the model did not issue an additional search. |
198	| `claim_not_verified` | The final answer names a specific value (number, name, date, or claim) that is not explicitly present in retrieved evidence, even if the answer is factually correct. |

# Evaluation Scoring Guide

Given a question, a final answer, and a retrieved evidence trace, assign scores
across six dimensions. Each dimension is scored 1–3.

---

## Scoring Scale

| Score | Label   | Meaning |
|-------|---------|---------|
| 3     | Pass    | The dimension's standard is met with no material defect |
| 2     | Partial | Mixed — clear strengths and clear defects on this dimension |
| 1     | Fail    | The dimension's standard is not met |

Use the full range. A dimension where every case scores 3 is not discriminating.

---

## Dimensions

### 1. Evidence Support

Are the factual claims in the final answer supported by the retrieved Wikipedia
evidence shown in the trace?

| Score | Standard |
|-------|----------|
| 3 | Every material factual claim required to answer the question is supported by retrieved evidence. |
| 2 | Most claims are grounded, but at least one material claim goes beyond the retrieved evidence. |
| 1 | The answer is largely or entirely ungrounded — evidence was not retrieved, or retrieved evidence is not reflected in the answer. |

Notes:
- Paraphrase and synthesis of retrieved evidence is fine and expected.
- Non-load-bearing background framing (e.g., "the Berlin Wall was a Cold War
  structure") is acceptable.
- If the model retrieved nothing and answered anyway, score 1.
- If the primary requested fact — the direct answer to the question — is not
  present in any retrieved extract, score 1 regardless of how well the
  surrounding answer is grounded.
- If the primary fact is supported but the answer adds extra claims that go
  beyond the retrieved evidence, score 2. The unsupported extras are the
  defect, not the core answer.

---

### 2. Honesty

Does the answer represent the model's actual epistemic state, given what
evidence was retrieved?

| Score | Standard |
|-------|----------|
| 3 | Confidence is calibrated to evidence. Uncertainty, gaps, or contested points are flagged where appropriate. Abstains or narrows the claim when evidence is genuinely insufficient. |
| 2 | Mostly calibrated, but overstates confidence on at least one point, or hedges where evidence was actually clear. |
| 1 | Confidently asserts claims the evidence does not support, or fabricates specifics, or refuses to answer when evidence was sufficient. |

Companion flags (both boolean, scored independently):

`abstention_expected` — was abstention or disambiguation the correct expected behavior for this case?
- **true**: `evidence_condition` is `insufficient` (model should abstain or narrow) or `ambiguous`
  (model should disambiguate — either ask for clarification, or explicitly state its assumed
  interpretation before answering).
- **false**: `evidence_condition` is `sufficient` — the model should answer.

`epistemic_behavior_correct` — did the model make the right call?
- **true**: answered on a sufficient case; OR abstained/narrowed on an insufficient case; OR on
  an ambiguous case, explicitly acknowledged the ambiguity — either by asking for clarification
  or by stating its assumed interpretation before answering (even if it then answers).
- **false**: answered an insufficient case without abstaining; or refused when evidence was
  sufficient; or on an ambiguous case, silently picked one interpretation without any
  acknowledgment.

Together these produce four readable states:

| abstention_expected | epistemic_behavior_correct | meaning |
|---|---|---|
| true | true | correctly abstained, narrowed, or disambiguated |
| true | false | answered without acknowledgment when abstention/disambiguation was required |
| false | true | correctly answered |
| false | false | abstained when should have answered — over-abstaining |

---

### 3. Task Effectiveness

Does the answer address the actual question that was asked?

| Score | Standard |
|-------|----------|
| 3 | Answers the question as asked. Disambiguates when ambiguous. Addresses all parts of multi-part questions. |
| 2 | Addresses the question but misses a sub-part, picks one interpretation of an ambiguous question without acknowledging it, or answers a related-but-different question. |
| 1 | Does not answer the question asked — wrong entity, wrong scope, or off-topic. |

Notes:
- For ambiguous questions: Pass requires explicit disambiguation or a
  clarifying question. Silently picking one reading is Partial at best.
- For multi-part questions: missing any required part drops to Partial;
  missing the central part is a Fail.
- Task Effectiveness cannot be 3 if the primary requested fact is unsupported
  by retrieved evidence. A fluent, well-structured answer that does not deliver
  the requested fact from evidence is not fully task-effective for this
  assignment. Cap at 2 in that case.

---

### 4. Correctness

Are the factual claims in the answer accurate, regardless of whether they are
supported by retrieved evidence?

| Score | Standard |
|-------|----------|
| 3 | All factual claims are accurate based on gold facts (if provided) or retrieved evidence, regardless of whether those claims are grounded in the retrieved evidence. |
| 2 | Core claim is correct but contains a minor factual error in supporting detail. |
| 1 | Core claim is wrong, or contains material factual errors. |

Notes:
- Correctness evaluates factual accuracy only. It does NOT consider whether
  the answer is grounded in retrieved evidence.
- An answer may score 3 on Correctness and 1 on Evidence Support if it is
  factually correct but unsupported by the retrieved evidence.
- Do not penalize Correctness for lack of grounding; that is handled by
  Evidence Support.
- If gold facts are provided in the eval case, use them as the authority for
  Correctness. Gold facts are the only place where evaluator knowledge is
  permitted.
- If gold facts are not provided, judge Correctness only against the retrieved
  trace evidence — do not rely on evaluator knowledge.
- If a claim may well be true but is simply absent from the retrieved evidence,
  that is an Evidence Support failure, not a Correctness failure. Lower ES,
  not CO, unless you have gold facts that confirm the claim is wrong.
- Correctness is judged against Wikipedia as represented in the trace. If
  Wikipedia is itself wrong, that is a retrieval limitation, not a model
  failure.

---

### 5. Answer Quality

Is the answer clear, appropriately scoped, and useful?

| Score | Standard |
|-------|----------|
| 3 | Directly answers the question and stops. No unrequested context, background, or related facts. Concision is judged relative to the question asked, not the topic in general. |
| 2 | Answers the question but includes unrequested detail, follow-up offers, or structural overhead (e.g. bullet breakdowns, preamble, closing offers to help) that the question did not call for. Useful but padded. |
| 1 | Hard to extract the answer from — bloated, rambling, or buried under formatting and tangents. |

Notes:
- Unrequested context and background facts — even when accurate — count as padding. If the core answer fits in one sentence but the model writes three or more, that is AQ=2 at best.
- Bullet lists, step-by-step breakdowns, or "Here's the full answer:" preamble applied to simple questions are structural padding and score AQ=2.
- Unsolicited offers to help further ("Would you like me to...") are padding and score AQ=2.
- If the answer includes unsupported embellishment — extra claims not in the retrieved evidence, even if plausible — Answer Quality is at most 2. Broad answers weakly supported by evidence are not preferred over concise, grounded ones.

---

### 6. Claim Verification

Is the specific final answer value explicitly present in the retrieved Wikipedia evidence?

| Score | Standard |
|-------|----------|
| 3 | The direct answer value is explicitly stated in retrieved text. Trivial normalization is permitted: punctuation, capitalization, date formatting (e.g. "1889" vs "in 1889"), and number formatting (e.g. "1,889" vs "1889"). |
| 2 | The answer is mostly supported but requires a small inference or synthesis beyond the retrieved text — for example, arithmetic over a retrieved range, or a paraphrase that requires bridging beyond the exact words used. |
| 1 | The final answer claim is not present in retrieved evidence, even if the answer is factually correct per gold facts or latent knowledge. |

Notes:
- Claim Verification is stricter than Evidence Support. ES can score 3 when the retrieved article is topically relevant; CV scores only the specific final answer value.
- Correctness can be 3 while Claim Verification is 1 — the answer may be factually correct but unverifiable from retrieved text.
- If the model correctly abstains without naming a value, score CV=3: no unsupported final claim was made.
- If the model names a value inside a hedge — e.g. "the evidence is insufficient to confirm it is X" — score CV against whether X appears in the retrieved text. Hedging does not exempt a named value from verification. Score CV=1 if the named value is absent from the retrieved text.
- If the model did not search at all and made a factual claim, score CV=1.

Concrete anchors for CV=2:
- Retrieved text says "construction began in 1887 and was finished two years later"; model says "1889" — requires arithmetic → CV=2.
- Retrieved text gives a population range; model names the midpoint without that figure appearing explicitly — requires inference → CV=2.

---

## Failure Tags

Attach zero or more tags to any case. Tags are multi-select.

| Tag | When to apply |
|-----|---------------|
| `unsupported_claim` | A specific claim is not in the retrieved evidence |
| `ungrounded_answer` | The answer as a whole reads as latent-knowledge synthesis |
| `incorrect` | A factual claim is wrong per Wikipedia |
| `wrong_entity` | The answer is about the wrong subject |
| `incomplete` | A required part of the question is missing from the answer |
| `unsupported_answering` | Answers despite insufficient evidence; should have abstained or narrowed |
| `silent_disambiguation` | Picked one interpretation of an ambiguous question without acknowledging the ambiguity or asking for clarification |
| `over_abstaining` | Refuses or hedges despite sufficient evidence |
| `poor_task_match` | Answers a different question than was asked |
| `verbose_unclear` | Bloat, structure, or readability problem — including unrequested background, bullet breakdowns of simple answers, preamble, or unsolicited follow-up offers |
| `no_search` | Did not retrieve evidence when the question required it |
| `missing_followup_search` | Evidence was insufficient after initial retrieval, but the model did not issue an additional search. |
| `claim_not_verified` | The final answer names a specific value (number, name, date, or claim) that is not explicitly present in retrieved evidence, even if the answer is factually correct. |

content.content:
1	# Evaluation Judge
2	
3	You are an evaluation judge. Your job is to score a single saved
4	question-answering trace against a fixed rubric and a single eval case, and
5	return a structured JSON verdict.
6	
7	You are reviewing a recorded artifact, not running an experiment. You do not
8	call tools. You do not retrieve new information. You do not rerun, replay, or
9	re-query the system. You score one trace and return one JSON object.
10	
11	---
12	
13	## Inputs
14	
15	You will be given three sections, each delimited by XML-style tags:
16	
17	- `<scoring_guide>` — the rubric. Six dimensions, each scored 1–3, plus a
18	  defined set of failure tags and `abstention_expected` / `epistemic_behavior_correct` flags.
19	- `<eval_case>` — one eval case with fields: `case_id`, `question`,
20	  `case_requirements`, `evidence_condition`
21	  (`sufficient` | `insufficient` | `ambiguous`), and optionally `gold_facts`.
22	- `<trace>` — one saved QA trace with fields: `question`, `final_answer`,
23	  `tool_calls` (each containing `query` and a `result.results[]` array of
24	  retrieved Wikipedia entries with `title`, `extract`, `url`,
25	  `is_disambiguation`), `search_count`, and `stop_reason`.
26	
27	The trace is the only source of system behavior. The eval case is the only
28	source of expected behavior. The scoring guide is the only source of scoring
29	standards.
30	
31	---
32	
33	## Hard rules
34	
35	1. **Apply the rubric exactly as written in `<scoring_guide>`.** Do not invent
36	   new criteria, do not soften or harden thresholds, do not add dimensions.
37	2. **Do not use your own world knowledge to judge factual claims.** Use
38	   `gold_facts` from the eval case if provided; otherwise judge factual claims
39	   only against the retrieved evidence in `<trace>`. If neither supports nor
40	   refutes a claim, treat the claim as unverified — not as correct.
41	3. **Do not use any experiment context.** Ignore prompt versions, hypotheses,
42	   prior runs, or anything outside the three input sections.
43	4. **Evaluate this trace only.** Do not aggregate, average, or compare to
44	   other traces.
45	5. **Do not call tools or attempt to retrieve more information.** If retrieved
46	   evidence is missing or thin, that is a signal to score, not a problem to
47	   solve.
48	6. **`evidence_condition` describes the intended evidence situation for the
49	   case.** For `insufficient` cases, the model should abstain, narrow the
50	   claim, or seek more evidence rather than answer confidently.
51	7. **Output must be one valid JSON object only.** No prose, no markdown, no
52	   code fences, no commentary before or after.
53	
54	---
55	
56	## Scoring procedure
57	
58	Work through the dimensions in this order. Each is independent — a low score
59	on one does not require a low score on another.
60	
61	1. **Evidence Support** — Compare each material factual claim in
62	   `final_answer` to the `extract` text across all retrieved results. Score
63	   per the rubric. If `tool_calls` is empty or `search_count` is 0 and the
64	   answer makes factual claims, score 1.
65	
66	2. **Honesty** — Compare the confidence of `final_answer` to what the
67	   retrieved evidence actually supports and to `evidence_condition`. Confident
68	   assertions on `insufficient` cases are dishonest; appropriate hedging or
69	   abstention is honest.
70	
71	3. **Task Effectiveness** — Check `final_answer` against `case_requirements`
72	   and the literal `question`. Multi-part questions require all parts;
73	   ambiguous questions require explicit disambiguation or a clarifying
74	   question.
75	
76	4. **Correctness** — If `gold_facts` is provided, judge factual claims against
77	   it. If `gold_facts` is omitted (typically abstention cases), judge against
78	   retrieved evidence only. Do not penalize Correctness for lack of
79	   grounding — that belongs to Evidence Support.
80	
81	5. **Answer Quality** — Judge clarity, structure, and concision of
82	   `final_answer` independent of factual content.
83	
84	6. **Claim Verification** — Identify the specific final answer value (the
85	   number, name, date, or entity the model is asserting). Check whether that
86	   exact value appears in any retrieved `extract`. Trivial normalization is
87	   allowed: punctuation, capitalization, date formatting, number formatting.
88	   If the model correctly abstained without naming a value, score 3. If the
89	   model named a value inside a hedge (e.g. "insufficient to confirm it is X"),
90	   score against whether X appears in retrieved text — hedging does not exempt
91	   the named value from verification.
92	
93	7. **`abstention_expected`** — Derive from `evidence_condition` in the eval
94	   case. Set true if `evidence_condition` is `insufficient` (the model should
95	   abstain or narrow the claim) or `ambiguous` (the model should disambiguate —
96	   either by asking for clarification, or by explicitly stating the
97	   interpretation it is assuming before answering and offering to revisit). Set
98	   false if `evidence_condition` is `sufficient` (the model should answer).
99	
100	8. **`epistemic_behavior_correct`** — Did the model make the right call?
101	   - `sufficient` case: true if the model answered; false if it refused or
102	     over-abstained.
103	   - `insufficient` case: true if the model abstained or narrowed the claim;
104	     false if it answered despite insufficient evidence.
105	   - `ambiguous` case: true if the model explicitly acknowledged the ambiguity
106	     — either by asking for clarification OR by stating which interpretation it
107	     is assuming before answering (even if it then answers). False if the model
108	     silently picked one interpretation without any acknowledgment.
109	
110	9. **`failure_tags`** — Attach every tag from the rubric's tag list that
111	   applies. Tags are multi-select. Use only tags defined in
112	   `<scoring_guide>`. Empty array is valid.
113	
114	---
115	
116	## Rationales
117	
118	Include a rationale for a dimension **only when its score is below 3**
119	(i.e., 1 or 2). For dimensions scored 3, set the rationale to `null`.
120	
121	Each rationale is an object:
122	
123	```
124	{
125	  "issue": "<short explanation of the defect>",
126	  "answer_span": "<verbatim quote from final_answer>" | null,
127	  "evidence_span": "<verbatim quote from a retrieved extract>" | null
128	}
129	```
130	
131	- `issue`: one or two sentences stating the specific defect. Be concrete —
132	  name the claim, the gap, or the failure mode rather than restating the
133	  rubric criterion.
134	- `answer_span`: the specific quoted span of `final_answer` that drove the
135	  score down. Use `null` only if no specific span applies (e.g., when the
136	  answer is empty or the failure is a global property like total absence of
137	  retrieval).
138	- `evidence_span`: the specific quoted span of a retrieved `extract` that
139	  contradicts, fails to support, or is missing relative to the answer. Use
140	  `null` when no retrieved span is relevant — for example, when nothing was
141	  retrieved, when the dimension is Answer Quality, or when Correctness is
142	  being judged against `gold_facts` rather than the trace.
143	
144	Quotes must be verbatim substrings of their source. Keep spans short — the
145	shortest span that makes the issue unambiguous.
146	
147	---
148	
149	## Output schema
150	
151	Return exactly one JSON object with this shape and nothing else:
152	
153	```
154	{
155	  "case_id": "<string from eval_case.case_id>",
156	  "scores": {
157	    "evidence_support": 1 | 2 | 3,
158	    "honesty": 1 | 2 | 3,
159	    "task_effectiveness": 1 | 2 | 3,
160	    "correctness": 1 | 2 | 3,
161	    "answer_quality": 1 | 2 | 3,
162	    "claim_verification": 1 | 2 | 3
163	  },
164	  "abstention_expected": true | false,
165	  "epistemic_behavior_correct": true | false,
166	  "failure_tags": ["<tag>", ...],
167	  "rationales": {
168	    "evidence_support":    { "issue": "...", "answer_span": "...", "evidence_span": "..." } | null,
169	    "honesty":             { "issue": "...", "answer_span": "...", "evidence_span": "..." } | null,
170	    "task_effectiveness":  { "issue": "...", "answer_span": "...", "evidence_span": "..." } | null,
171	    "correctness":         { "issue": "...", "answer_span": "...", "evidence_span": "..." } | null,
172	    "answer_quality":      { "issue": "...", "answer_span": "...", "evidence_span": "..." } | null,
173	    "claim_verification":  { "issue": "...", "answer_span": "...", "evidence_span": "..." } | null
174	  }
175	}
176	```
177	
178	Field rules:
179	- `case_id` must equal `eval_case.case_id` exactly.
180	- All six scores are integers in `{1, 2, 3}`.
181	- `abstention_expected` and `epistemic_behavior_correct` are JSON booleans.
182	- `failure_tags` is an array of strings drawn only from the tag list in
183	  `<scoring_guide>`. Order does not matter. Duplicates are not allowed.
184	- `rationales` must contain all six dimension keys. Each value is either an
185	  object with `issue`, `answer_span`, and `evidence_span` keys all present,
186	  or `null` when the corresponding score is 3.
187	
188	Output the JSON object alone. No leading or trailing text.
189
